items.title: Stop Overpaying for EBS I The Keys to AWS Optimization | S16 E3

ai.summary: Based on the available description… This livestream is a practical deep dive into Amazon EBS cost optimization, showing how to identify overprovisioned volumes, right-size storage, and adopt newer EBS volume types to align performance with cost. It also covers removing waste like unused snapshots and outdated volumes, using monitoring tools, and real-world examples with actionable strategies to cut EBS spending without sacrificing performance.
bullets: Identify and right‑size overprovisioned EBS volumes
Leverage the latest EBS volume types to match performance and cost
Eliminate waste from unused snapshots, outdated volumes, and inefficient configs
Use monitoring tools to track storage utilization
Real-world examples and actionable strategies to reduce EBS spending without losing performance
takeaways: Right‑sizing and choosing appropriate EBS volume types can significantly lower storage costs while preserving required performance
Implement lifecycle practices to remove unused snapshots and outdated volumes to stop silent spending drains
Invest in monitoring storage utilization to find and prioritize optimization opportunities
Apply the demonstrated, actionable strategies to reduce EBS spend without impacting service delivery

items.title: How I turned $27K of land into $700/night luxury cabin rentals

ai.summary: Based on the available description, full-time contractor Chris Broomfield bought a five-acre property in upstate New York in 2015 and transformed a $27K land purchase into luxury Airbnb cabins charging about $700 per night. He built an initial A-frame cabin himself over weekend commutes from Connecticut, added additional cabins over time, and by September 2025 the property had generated over $2.1 million.
bullets: 2015 purchase of a five-acre lot (land cited as $27K) in upstate New York to develop Airbnb properties.
Chris Broomfield, a full-time contractor, commuted from Connecticut on weekends to build an A-frame cabin over three years.
Additional cabins were added after the initial build, creating multiple short-term rental units.
Cabins are marketed as luxury rentals commanding about $700 per night.
By September 2025 the property had produced over $2.1 million in revenue.
takeaways: High-value short-term rental conversions on rural land can create concentrated demand for reliable power and connectivity.
Incremental, owner-driven development suggests infrastructure needs may grow gradually as properties scale.
Service providers and planners should consider rural hospitality use cases when assessing capacity and redundancy requirements.
Opportunities exist to offer targeted solutions (backup power, robust broadband, remote monitoring) to support high-end remote rentals.

items.title: Restrukturyzacja a podatki. Rozpoznanie, obowiązki, konsekwencje | Monika Palmowska, Tomasz Regulski

ai.summary: Based on the available description, the podcast explains how to identify when operational changes (like moving production, distribution or teams) amount to a restructuring from a transfer pricing and valuation perspective, what reporting obligations may arise, and why timely documentation matters. Experts Monika Palmowska and Tomasz Regulski walk through recognition, EBIT analysis, valuation roles, common pitfalls, and a practical checklist for firms.
bullets: Defines restructuring in transfer pricing and how to spot material changes.
Explains analyzing EBIT impact and preparing restructuring documentation.
Covers reporting duties including TPR and MDR and common valuation mistakes.
Gives examples: moving production/distribution, transferring employees, and contract transfers.
Ends with a practical checklist of actions for companies undergoing change.
takeaways: When relocating production, distribution or teams, assess if changes trigger transfer pricing restructuring rules and related reporting.
Prepare transfer-pricing and valuation documentation on time to reduce future compliance risk and stress.
Analyze EBIT shifts to determine materiality and support restructuring decisions.
Review potential reporting obligations (TPR, MDR) early in the process to ensure timely filings.
Use objective valuations and watch for common valuation/reporting errors highlighted by the experts.
